text:
Once upon a time, there was a young fox who loved collecting beautiful feathers. Every day after school, he would hop through the trees, weaving through fields of vibrant wildflowers. His favorite place was a glade where a flock of glossy owls seldom flew. It was here that he discovered a mysterious old owl perched on a gnarled oak.

The owl hadn't sung. Its feathers were feathers of dark storms.

"My dear," it spoke quietly, a voice like gentle rain. "These feathers are as beautiful as any song. But you cannot own them."

"You? You fly like the wind!" exclaimed the fox.

"The wind does not own beauty, forest child. Only birds like you."

The fox thought for a moment. "Sometimes I wish I could fly. Just once."

"You would sing differently then—a song of longing. So stay here."

Each evening, the owl taught the fox a new tune. It was an old ocean song, sad yet beautiful.
